narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and we appreciate the journey that's brought you here. The admissions model we use predicted a 63.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which unfortunately crossed our threshold for approving applications—leading to the rejection of yours. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings from earlier years in school, where you placed in the lowest decile for both Year 1 and Year 3 grades, far below the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. These early academic struggles were the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure, as they stood out as the most influential elements in your profile compared to others. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA was close to the average of 3.21, which actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing the bar. Your lower-middle family income background also helped a bit, offering a protective influence that many applicants from similar circumstances benefit from in these models. Even your LSAT score of 28, though below the dataset average of about 37, provided a small positive pull toward success, and your choice of full-time study aligned with the vast majority of students who succeed. Overall, while your recent GPA and background softened the edges, those low early decile rankings dominated the decision, setting you apart from typical applicants who tend to have stronger consistent academic showings. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background—many in similar situations have turned things around with targeted preparation. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your early academic record through additional coursework or certifications if possible, and perhaps exploring part-time options or support programs for students from lower-middle income families. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift the model's view dramatically in your favor. Hang in there—you've got the foundation to build on.
